title: Closing Bell: Near-zero rates are closer than you think
full_text:
The Reserve Bank of Australia talking about what to do when interest rates are approaching zero and it needs to stimulate the economy seems naff given the cash rate's general direction. Until you consider two things.
Historically, advanced economies cut rates by about 400 basis points during recessions. And the cash rate is at only 4.35 per cent.
That 4.35 per cent is supposed to be restrictive territory - the neutral rate is probably more like 3.6 per cent.
So any time there is a recession - and with the RBA forecasting only 1.3 to 1.4 per cent a year GDP growth in each of the next two years, we shouldn't rule it out - we could be back to zero or near-zero rates in no time.
Challenger's Jonathan Kearns pointed this out at a pretty wonky RBA briefing on Monday.
It was a bit weird talking about near-zero rates and what to do next given Australia's inflation problem and expectations we could get a fourth rate hike this year, before Kearns' reminder that neutral rates had dropped so low that the prospect for near-zero rates was only ever just around the corner.
Which gave the required urgency to RBA assistant governor Christopher Kent's new playbook for what to do when rates are low and more monetary policy tools are needed.
This framework isn't something that will necessarily collect dust for years; this conversation that felt like it was from another era - the pandemic and pre-pandemic era, which resulted in the RBA review and made this framework a mandatory exercise - is timeless.
Kent made it clear that the cash rate is "the absolute most important tool" for the RBA. But it has to properly think and prepare for other tools given how the bank was caught on the hop during the pandemic, and reached for unconventional policy like forward guidance for the first time.
Kearns, who was still at the RBA during the pandemic, agrees.
"As much as people had read the literature, there wasn't really a domestic playbook to use," he says.
Although the cash rate is the primary method, the new framework ranks the various other tools - the ones RBA reached for in the pandemic and the ones it did not - under various scenarios: GFC-style financial system dysfunction, real economy shock, slow grind-down and mixed supply/demand shock. Barrenjoey's Jo Masters flipped straight to the tools heat map, which ranked them all.
Term lending facilities were listed as the preferred method for all four scenarios, followed by government bond purchases, forward guidance and even negative interest rate policy, which Kent says isn't preferred but also couldn't be ruled out. The probability of term-rate targets and FX asset purchases was "low" across each scenario.
So, Masters understandably asked, are term lending facilities the first choice tool as the heat map suggests?
Kent says it's the cash rate target first, pre-emptively cutting the cash rate target second to avoid the need for further cuts, and then it depends on the circumstances.
He says Australia's bank-heavy funding markets mean term lending facilities such as the infamous term funding facility (TFF) that offered cheap debt to lenders in the pandemic "can be very powerful" (in Kent's words), but they won't always be the first choice of tool, suggesting this framework is more central bank guide than RBA bible.
So it is a bit rubbery, but Masters says the framework is useful - central banks, investors and economists are getting hit with shocks almost constantly, everyone's trying to learn from lessons of the not-too-distant past, so it is helpful to have the guidelines the monetary policy and governance boards will consider the next time the economy is flashing red.
It is arguably more important now that we have those separate monetary policy and governance boards - the first whose job in a crisis will be to look after the economy, and the second it is to think about the central bank's balance sheet and risk. It's easy to see how the two could clash.
Kent said RBA staff and the two boards will be put through "fire drills" on the framework to make sure they can all act quicker next time a crisis rolls around and not stutter their way into a deeper hole.
All involved will war-game economic shocks and how to respond: What are the tools? What are the exit strategy from using the tools? What does each of them mean for the economy and financial markets more broadly? And how will they be communicated?
The only thing that matters is that it makes for better decision-making, and it is hard to see how preparing for such conversations could make the RBA's response any worse.
But the real purpose here is to show us the RBA has learnt - or is willing to try to learn - from the pandemic.
Hindsight makes this observation easy, but the economy came out of the pandemic way too hot - inflation has been above target for years and could remain so for another two years. There was no need for the central bank to extend the TFF when funding markets were not closed for the banks for example, especially while the government's JobKeeper scheme pumped the economy full of cash and vaccinations were coming.
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Anthony Macdonald
heading: Banking and Economy
